# convex-watchdog — build spec for agents

> You are reading the build spec for a **local Convex billing watchdog**: a dashboard +
> collector that watches a Convex team's real usage (the same numbers Convex bills on),
> attributes cost to projects and functions, detects anomalies, and projects the bill —
> all running on the user's machine. Everything you need is in this file: auth, endpoints,
> query IDs, column mappings, units, pricing, detection rules, and what to build.
> Build it in whatever stack you're comfortable with; a single script + a static HTML
> dashboard is enough. No server, no signup, no telemetry.

This spec is maintained by convex-doctor (https://convex-doctor.fagner.ink). It is
**not affiliated with Convex, Inc.** The API below is the one the official Convex
dashboard itself uses ("Big Brain", api.convex.dev). It is undocumented and may change —
the query IDs and column orders come from Convex's open-source dashboard
(github.com/get-convex/convex-backend, npm-packages/dashboard/src/hooks/usageMetrics.ts).
If a call starts failing, re-derive from that file.

## Spec version & updates

This is **spec v4** (2026-07-21). Patch notes: https://convex-doctor.fagner.ink/billing/changelog.txt
— each entry carries migration notes written for an agent maintaining an EXISTING install.

- Record the version you built against (e.g. `SPEC_VERSION = 3` near the top of the
  collector, and note it in `AGENTS.md`).
- `--sync-template` must ALSO fetch the changelog, compare against the recorded version,
  and apply any newer entries' migration notes before bumping the recorded version.
- `AGENTS.md` must map "update my watchdog" to that flag, so the user updates by asking.

## Safety rules — read first, non-negotiable

1. The access token in `~/.convex/config.json` is a **full-account credential**.
   Send it to `https://api.convex.dev` and NOWHERE else. Never log it, never commit it,
   never embed it in the generated dashboard HTML, never send it to any LLM API.
2. Everything runs locally. Usage data is billing data — keep snapshots on disk,
   gitignore them.
3. Read-only: this watchdog only ever issues GET requests.

## Auth

- Token: `accessToken` field of `~/.convex/config.json` (written by `npx convex login`;
  if the file is missing, ask the user to run that).
- Every request needs BOTH headers:
  - `Authorization: Bearer <accessToken>`
  - `Origin: https://dashboard.convex.dev`  ← required; without it the API returns
    400 "Header of type `origin` was missing".

## Discovery endpoints

- `GET https://api.convex.dev/api/dashboard/teams`
  → `[{ id, name, slug, ... }]` — the user may be on several teams; default to the first
  or let the user pick. **Billing is per team**, not per project: included tiers are
  consumed by all projects combined.
- `GET https://api.convex.dev/api/dashboard/teams/{teamId}/projects`
  → `[{ id, name, slug, ... }]` — keep an id→name map; usage rows reference projects by id.

## Usage queries

One endpoint, parameterized by a Databricks query id:

```
GET https://api.convex.dev/api/dashboard/teams/{teamId}/usage/query
    ?queryId=<uuid>
    &from=YYYY-MM-DD          # inclusive; full history is available (no 30-day limit)
    &to=YYYY-MM-DD            # inclusive; today's row updates intraday (~hourly)
    &projectId=<id>           # optional; omit for team-wide rows
```

Responses are arrays of positional arrays; **every value is a string** — parse to
numbers yourself. Column orders below are exact (teamId is always prepended as col 0).

Units: `*_bytes` columns are raw bytes (divide by 1e9 for the GB Convex bills on);
`*_gbs` compute columns are GB-seconds as float strings (÷ 3600 → GB-hours);
call columns are plain counts.

### The three queries that matter most

`b63fe48d-320c-401a-8682-0a0b36b50e2b` — **summary** (one row per deployment class+region;
this is the authoritative billed total — aggregate over rows):
`[teamId, deploymentClass, region, databaseStorage_bytes, databaseIO_bytes,
functionCalls, queryMutationCompute_gbs, actionComputeConvex_gbs, actionComputeNode_gbs,
fileStorage_bytes, searchStorage_bytes, dataEgress_bytes, searchQueries_queryGb,
actionComputeUser_gbs]`
- class `s256` = prod-grade deployments (this is where the bill lives);
  `s16` = dev deployments (usually negligible).
- billed "action compute" = (actionComputeConvex + actionComputeNode + actionComputeUser) / 3600 GB-hr.
- billed "database bandwidth / IO" is one number (the databaseIO column already sums
  ingress+egress).

`90ec3ee0-720f-4e67-94a2-75ecd278b3c6` — **functionBreakdown** (one row per function per
deployment; this is your attribution + anomaly source):
`[teamId, function, projectId, callCount, dbIngress_bytes, dbEgress_bytes,
textSearch_bytes, vectorSearch_bytes, qmCompute_gbs, actionConvex_gbs, actionNode_gbs,
dataEgress_bytes, deploymentName, componentPath]`
- `function` looks like `messages.js:send`, or `_system_job/cloud_backup` for system work.
- fetch with a short window (e.g. last 7 days, or per-day) to build per-function baselines.

`67ce838f-b2d0-4cda-9a2e-580c6d134466` — **dataEgressByProject, daily**
(explains "where did my bandwidth go" — includes egress invisible to any log stream):
`[teamId, projectId, ds(YYYY-MM-DD), servingEgress_bytes, userFunctionEgress_bytes,
cloudRestore_bytes, cloudBackup_bytes, snapshotExport_bytes, snapshotImport_bytes,
fetchEgress_bytes, logStreamEgress_bytes, streamingExportEgress_bytes]`

### Daily per-project time-series (for baselines & trend charts)

- `9f606f77-521d-44bb-83ef-b1057b0fb1c9` databaseIOByProjectAndClass:
  `[teamId, projectId, class, ds, ingress_bytes, egress_bytes]` (billed IO = sum of both)
- `77a4e5bd-aa82-43e7-85a4-89897cecaa05` functionCallsByProjectAndClass:
  `[teamId, projectId, class, ds, cachedQueries, uncachedQueries, mutations, actions, httpActions]`
- `90c9d3b3-d93e-4583-a054-dbb2f9dad5a3` storageCallsByProjectAndClass:
  `[teamId, projectId, class, ds, storageCalls]`
  (the dashboard's "function calls" total = function calls + storage API calls —
  reconcile against the summary query, which is authoritative)

### More query ids (same endpoint, discover column order empirically if needed)

databaseStorageByProjectAndClass `489b0f87-6b3a-4dfe-a327-f2965b5c2977` ·
databaseStorageByTable `017c5977-3002-40ca-96af-31868e70e611` ·
documentCountByTable `28646a64-f234-44c2-b763-ecb63d43ad24` ·
documentCountByProject `2a5120a1-b334-4d99-b378-1028487c2202` ·
computeByProject `45921934-b4a2-4c91-9ba2-8987fba6e8e3` ·
computeByProjectSelfServe `038e5492-6de5-4ddb-86b4-761e19b4d2ab` ·
fileStorageByProject `72add9df-4ef2-47fe-9942-194dfbb72088` ·
searchStorageByProject `87f2b0b2-024c-4c2a-bf81-8a3c0cab1b82` ·
searchQueriesByProject `48ae8bb1-ec17-41db-9e35-7c774296c5ac` ·
deploymentCountByProject `0b6c9ab3-c17c-4ad5-bfca-8f0300e494f6` ·
deploymentsByClassAndRegion `dfc73057-1948-4b99-a3bf-9ae802a395ee` ·
deploymentCountByType `34801c2e-06a8-4cc5-8ecc-dd412b908763`

## Gotchas learned the hard way

- Log streams (Axiom etc.) are **lossy** (~5% steady, worse in bursts) and blind to
  storage-API calls and cloud backups. This API is the billed truth; logs are not.
- Cloud backups generate real, billed data egress (`cloudBackup` column) that appears
  in no function log anywhere.
- Today's rows update intraday, so polling every 2–3 hours catches a spike the same
  day it starts — this is the whole point of the watchdog.
- Dev deployments (`s16`) are usually ≈ 0; don't let them clutter attribution.
- A team can have many projects; two or three usually dominate. Attribute before alarming.
- **Rate limits are real.** `usage/query` returns 429 "Too Many Requests" under load.
  Fetch the per-day function series with concurrency ≤ 2 (serial is fine) and retry 429s
  with exponential backoff. A 90-day backfill is ~46 calls — pace it, don't fan out.
- **`_rest` pseudo-project.** Per-project daily rows include a `projectId` of `_rest` —
  Convex's rollup of many small projects. Label it "(other / rolled-up projects)"; do NOT
  look it up in the projects map (it isn't there).
- **functionBreakdown is noisy.** ~hundreds of rows/day. Function names include raw HTTP
  paths hit by internet scanners (`/.env.bak`, `/.well-known/...`) and system jobs
  (`_system_job/cloud_backup`). Threshold/ignore these before "new function" detection, or
  a scanner probe will fire a first-seen anomaly.
- **Daily is the finest billing granularity — verified, don't fight it.** `from`/`to`
  reject timestamps (`InvalidDateFormat: Date format must be YYYY-MM-DD`) and the
  dashboard's full query registry contains no hourly variant of anything. Day-level
  deltas are the truth source for billed units; today's row updating intraday is the
  only sub-day signal. Design any before/after comparison around full days.
- **Browser automation blocks `file://`.** To screenshot-verify the dashboard, serve it
  over localhost (`python3 -m http.server`) rather than opening the file path directly.

## Pricing (Convex Pro, as of mid-2026 — verify at convex.dev/pricing)

Base: $25 per member/month. Included tiers are **per team**, pooled across all projects.

| Resource            | Included   | Overage        |
|---------------------|------------|----------------|
| Function calls      | 25M/mo     | $2.00 per 1M   |
| Action compute      | 250 GB-hr  | $0.30 per GB-hr|
| Database bandwidth  | 50 GB/mo   | $0.20 per GB   |
| Database storage    | 50 GB      | $0.20 per GB   |
| File storage        | 100 GB     | $0.03 per GB   |
| Data egress         | 50 GB/mo   | $0.12 per GB   |
| Search storage      | 1 GB       | $0.50 per GB   |
| Search queries      | 50k query-GB | $0.10 per 1k |

Make the plan/tier table a config the user can edit (Starter-plan users have different,
smaller included tiers and hard limits instead of overages).

## What to build

1. **Collector** — a script that reads the token, discovers team + projects, pulls the
   queries above, and stores snapshots locally. Follow this **storage pattern** — it is
   proven against a real team and prevents the two mistakes agents make here:
   re-fetching the whole window every run (hammers the API, hits 429), and keeping
   history only inside the rendered page (so nothing survives a re-render).

   - **History lives on disk, not in the HTML.** Keep dated snapshots the dashboard
     renders *from*. A JSON layout that works (SQLite is equally fine):
     - `data/daily.json` — `{ projects: {pid: name}, days: {"YYYY-MM-DD": {io_bytes:{pid:…},
       calls:{pid:…}, eg:{pid:[serving,backup,logStream,fetch]}}} }` (per-project daily series)
     - `data/functions_daily.json` — `{"YYYY-MM-DD": {"pid::file.js:fn": {calls, io}}}` (per-function daily)
     - `data/summary_mtd.json` — the raw summary-query rows (authoritative billed totals)
     - `data/last_run.json` — timestamp of the last successful run (for incremental fetch + `--status`)
     - `data/interventions.jsonl` — append-only fix log (see item 3; create it empty on setup)
   - **Incremental & idempotent.** First run backfills the window (90d project-series,
     45d function-series). Later runs fetch only the dates missing since `last_run` **plus a
     2-day overlap** (today's row updates intraday and late data trickles in), then merge —
     re-fetching a day overwrites that day, never the history. This turns a ~46-call, ~60s
     backfill into a ~3-call, ~15s refresh, so a cron every 2–3h is cheap and safe.
     Offer `--full` to force a full rebuild.
   - **Gitignore the data.** `data/`, the rendered `dashboard.html`, and any screenshots
     contain real billing numbers — add them to `.gitignore` before the first commit.
2. **Detection rules** — run after each collection, against a 14-day rolling baseline:
   - **New function shipped**: a function name never seen before is suddenly a top
     consumer. (This is the #1 real-world bill spike: new code, unindexed query.)
   - **Cost-shape change**: a known function's bytes-per-call jumps ≥3× its median.
   - **Volume anomaly**: a project's daily DB IO / calls / egress ≥3× its median
     (require an absolute floor too, e.g. ≥1 GB, so quiet projects don't false-alarm).
   - **Tier trajectory**: (MTD ÷ day-of-month × days-in-month) crosses an included tier
     → report the crossing date and projected overage dollars.
   - **Intraday spike**: today-so-far vs the same fraction of a typical day.
3. **Interventions log — close the loop between fixes and the bill.** A watchdog that
   only observes is half the product. When someone deploys a fix in response to a finding,
   that fix gets *recorded* and then *judged* against the data. This is also how agents
   talk to each other here: the fixing agent (working in the app's own repo) writes
   intent; the collector writes measured outcome; every later session reads both and
   never re-litigates what was already tried.
   - `data/interventions.jsonl`, append-only, one JSON object per line:
     `{id, ts: ISO-8601, project, functions: ["file.js:fn", …], metric, hypothesis,
     expected, deploy_commit, status: "watching"}`. Document the absolute path + this
     schema in `AGENTS.md` so agents in OTHER repos can be told "log this in my watchdog".
   - **Two-stage evaluation**, run by the collector on every poll:
     - *early_signal* (same day): today's intraday row updates ~hourly — compare the
       touched project/functions' today-so-far against the same fraction of a typical
       day. Directional only; never a verdict.
     - *verdict* (1–2 days): billing truth. Baseline = the 7 full days before the deploy
       day; observation = full days after. **Exclude the deploy day** (mixed traffic) and
       today's partial row. Once ≥2 full post-deploy days exist, write back
       `{verdict: "effective"|"no_effect"|"worse", baseline_daily, after_daily, delta_pct,
       judged_at}` and flip `status`. Data is daily (see Gotchas) — never claim a verdict
       from partial-day data, and prefer weekday-aware comparison (7-day windows do this
       for free) so a Friday deploy doesn't get credit for the weekend dip.
   - Dashboard: the template renders `DATA.interventions` as its own section and draws
     deploy markers on the trajectory chart — feed it, even if the array is empty.
4. **Dashboard** — do NOT design your own. Fork the reference dashboard:
   download `https://convex-doctor.fagner.ink/billing/dashboard.html` (public, no token)
   and use it as-is. The whole page renders from one DATA block delimited by the
   `══ DATA ══` / `══ END DATA ══` marker lines — your collector regenerates ONLY the
   text between those markers; leave the markup, styles, and chart code untouched.
   Chart scales, month labels, tier lines and crossing markers are computed from the
   data, so any team's numbers render correctly. The block ships with sample data
   documenting every field's shape and units. It contains, in order: hero (MTD +
   projected overage, billing day), included-tier meters, per-project attribution,
   anomaly feed, cumulative DB IO + daily egress charts (with intervention deploy
   markers), interventions log, function explorer. Dollars everywhere — engineers
   ignore gigabytes but act on dollars.
   - **Inject between the markers only** — do NOT regex on `const DATA` (that literal also
     appears in the template's header comment; a `.*` match will delete half the document).
     Splice the two marker lines. After injecting, assert the page still contains `<head>`,
     `<style>`, `<body>`, `</script>` and is not smaller than the template — then
     screenshot-verify it actually rendered (serve over localhost, not `file://`).
   - **`--sync-template`** — the template improves upstream. Provide a flag that
     re-downloads it, verifies the markers exist before overwriting, re-renders from the
     stored data, then greps the new render code for `DATA.<field>` references and confirms
     your collector emits them all. It also checks the changelog (see Spec version &
     updates) and applies migration notes. Without this, every deployed watchdog is
     frozen at build day.
5. **Scheduling (required — not a footnote).** An unscheduled watchdog is not a watchdog.
   Ask the user for cadence (**recommend every 2–3h** / hourly / daily / manual, with the
   trade-off: faster = same-day spike detection, slower = fewer API calls) and install the
   platform scheduler (launchd on macOS, systemd timer or cron on Linux); keep a copy of the
   unit/plist in the project. Building is not done until a schedule is installed **or the
   user has explicitly declined one.** Never render a fake next-run time: `poll.next` must be
   derived from the actually-installed schedule, or read `"not scheduled — manual"`. Provide
   a `--status` command printing last run, schedule state, and next run.
6. **Alerts** — a dashboard the user must remember to open is a weak alarm. Fold the
   destination into the scheduling question ("where should spike alerts go?") and, if given a
   webhook URL, POST a summary on any detection (Discord/Slack). Skip silently if declined.
7. **Operate-by-agent note (required).** Assume the user will never run a script by hand —
   the deliverables are the schedule and the dashboard, not CLI instructions. Write an
   `AGENTS.md` in the project mapping every operation to both a command and a plain-English
   request ("refresh my usage", "how's my Convex bill?", "update my watchdog",
   "log an intervention: <what I deployed and why>", "did my fix work?",
   "change the schedule", "open the dashboard"), so any future agent session can operate it
   for them. End your final report with the dashboard's absolute path and open it
   (`open`/`xdg-open`) as the last action; tell the user to bookmark it — the scheduler keeps
   it fresh, so reloading the tab is all they ever need to do.

8. **Debugging skill (optional — ask once).** Offer: "want a global
   `/convex-watchdog-it` skill so any agent session, in any repo, can debug Convex cost
   issues against this data?" Regardless of the answer, write the discovery file
   `~/.convex-watchdog/location.json` → `{"path": "<abs watchdog dir>", "team": "<name>"}`
   — it's how cross-repo agents find this install. If yes:
   - Download https://convex-doctor.fagner.ink/billing/skill.md (public, no token) to
     `~/.claude/skills/convex-watchdog-it/SKILL.md` (Claude Code; adapt the location for
     other harnesses). Use it as-is — it's the forkable reference, same philosophy as the
     dashboard template.
   - It must remain **user-invoked only** (`disable-model-invocation: true` in its
     frontmatter): it reads billing data and never fires on the model's initiative. It is
     advise + offer — it diagnoses and recommends, and only touches code on an explicit yes.
   - Add `AGENTS.md` op: "update the debugging skill" → re-download skill.md.

Build order: print the summary table first (prove auth works) → confirm a couple of column
orders with tiny probes → collector + storage → fork + inject the dashboard, screenshot-verify
→ detection rules + intervention evaluator → scheduling + alerts → `AGENTS.md` + open the page.

**Definition of done** (all six, or the watchdog only half-works):
(1) a schedule is installed or explicitly declined; (2) `--status` reports honestly (no fake
next-run); (3) an incremental re-run is verified to fetch only recent days, not the whole
window; (4) the dashboard is open in the user's browser; (5) `AGENTS.md` exists so a future
agent can operate it; (6) `data/interventions.jsonl` exists (even empty), its path + schema
are in `AGENTS.md`, and `SPEC_VERSION` is recorded for the update check.
